summary: O'Neil announces her upcoming talk at Harvard's Data Privacy Lab, where she'll present a technical version of her 'Weapons of Math Destruction' thesis and propose technological tools for auditing opaque algorithms. She emphasizes measuring algorithm outputs rather than scrutinizing source code as an approach to understanding black-box systems. The post highlights her interest in meeting Latanya Sweeney, who as FTC chief technologist can advise on what tools regulators might actually adopt. O'Neil expresses a practical concern about developing useful tools rather than academic exercises.
key_points: Proposes examining black-box algorithms by measuring outputs rather than scrutinizing source code
Defines 'Weapons of Math Destruction' as opaque, widespread, and destructive algorithms
Seeks regulator input to ensure developed tools will actually be adopted
Notes a gap between what technologists can build and what regulators know is possible
money_quotes: I want to examine the question of how we understand a black-box algorithm in terms of measuring its outputs (as opposed to scrutinizing the source code).
I don't want to spend 4 years developing tools that nobody would use.
I have the impression that they generally speaking don't know what kind of tools are possible.
key_insight: Effective algorithmic accountability requires bridging the gap between what technologists can build and what regulators know to ask for.
